topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of Dr. Elias Thorne’s office felt cold against my palms. I stood by the reception desk, clutching the strap of my worn backpack so tightly my knuckles were white. He was sitting behind the expansive desk, a picture of serene composure, his gaze warm and utterly devoid of the sharp mockery I remembered from the schoolyard. He smiled, a gentle, knowing curve of his lips, and offered a quiet greeting. My throat tightened; the air in the room seemed too thin to draw a full breath. Every instinct screamed at me to bolt, to find an exit, but my feet felt rooted to the expensive carpet. I managed a strangled whisper, "Hello, Doctor." The sound felt foreign, brittle.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, perched on a velvet armchair, as he spoke to a nervous young patient. The way he modulated his voice, the precise, empathetic cadence—it was all him, yet entirely transformed. A tremor ran through my hands, making the cheap plastic pen in my pocket rattle. I tried to focus on the muted colors of the room, on the way the afternoon light slanted across the floor, anything to keep my attention from the unsettling realization that the architect of my worst memories was now the one gently dismantling them. When he finally looked up, catching my eye, there was a flicker—a ghost of that old sneer—before the practiced calm settled back over his features. I quickly looked down at my shoes, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The initial consultation was a tightrope walk. I sat on the edge of the sofa, my posture rigid, every muscle coiled. He leaned forward, his expression intensely focused, and asked a probing question about my early experiences. The memory surfaced—the sharp sting of his words, the way he’d made me feel small. A sudden, involuntary shiver traced its way down my spine. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my mouth making the action difficult. "It's... strange," I managed, my voice pitched higher than intended. I watched his reaction closely; a subtle tightening around his eyes suggested he understood the weight of what I was admitting. The silence stretched, heavy and suffocating, as I waited for the next move, my entire body braced for impact.
